subject: Statistics
title: A/B Testing: Experiments on the Web
description: A micro-lesson that makes A/B Testing: Experiments on the Web usable.

hook.text: Google tested 41 shades of blue for a link color. The winner earned an extra $200 million per year.

buildup.text: A/B testing randomly shows different website versions to visitors and measures which performs better.

discovery.text: With millions of users, even tiny differences become statistically significant. A 0.1% improvement matters at scale.

twist.text: Peeking at results early inflates false positives. Many companies stop tests too soon and celebrate noise.

climax.text: Sequential testing and Bayesian methods fix the peeking problem. Modern A/B testing is becoming more sophisticated.

punchline.text: Every click is data. Every user is a participant. The web experiments on us.

quiz.question: What problem does 'peeking' cause in A/B testing?
options: Checking results early inflates false positive rates — teams may celebrate noise as a real effect
It causes the website to crash
Peeking makes the test run longer than necessary
